FT_IC1.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract mentions ABCD Study but cannot verify U.S. vs other cohort without full paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: No PDF. Abstract mentions 'recreational screen time' but cannot confirm smartphone/social-media specificity without full text.

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available to verify statistical tests reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available to verify numeric coefficients.

FT_IC5.evidence: No PDF to confirm study design.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, The Lancet Child & Adolescent Health, 2018.

FT_EC7_reason: Cannot assess without full paper PDF.
notes: Paper requires PDF to complete screening. Abstract insufficient for FT_IC1-IC5 determination.

FT_IC1.evidence: Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) Study, the Netherlands. This is Dutch ABCD, not U.S. ABCD. Methods: 'Cross-sectional study on children aged 5-6 years from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study, the Netherlands (n=2354).'

FT_IC2.evidence: Personal cell phone and cordless phone use measured. Methods: 'residential presence of indoor sources (cordless phone base stations and Wi-Fi) and children's cell phone and cordless phone use was reported by the mother' and categorical frequency of 'both phone uses' assessed.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models testing associations between RF-EMF exposure sources and cognitive function measures (speed of information processing, inhibitory control, cognitive flexibility, visuomotor coordination). Multiple statistical tests reported with significance levels.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients and p-values reported for associations. Results section: 'Residential presence of RF-EMF indoor sources was associated with an improved speed of information processing' with multiple associations reported for different exposure sources and cognitive outcomes.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis. Methods: 'Cross-sectional study on children aged 5-6 years from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study' with linear regression analyses performed.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Environmental Research, Volume 150, October 2016, Pages 364-374.

FT_EC7_reason: FAILS FT_IC1 (NOT U.S. ABCD). FT_EC7 assessment moot as paper does not meet inclusion criterion.
notes: Paper meets FT_IC2-IC6 but is excluded due to FT_IC1 failure (Dutch ABCD, not U.S. ABCD).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study. Methods: 'The ongoing ABCD Study collected demographic and screen use data (among other domains) from 11 878 children and primary caregivers at baseline (mean age = 9.9 years) beginning in 2016 with annual follow-up assessments. The analytic sample included all participants with complete data (N = 9475).'

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership age as specific exposure variable. Methods: 'Parents were asked each year at what age their child received their first smartphone.' Outcome coded as 'Early smartphone ownership (<10 years old)' modeled as binary outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and smartphone ownership age. Methods: 'We then estimated a cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression model to examine associations between key sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, and parental education) and first smartphone ownership age.'

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2. Example: Female OR = 1.38 (95% CI 1.19-1.60); Black OR = 2.78 (95% CI 2.07-3.64); Single parent OR = 2.01 (95% CI 1.67-2.44); all p < .05 or p < .001 noted.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Methods describe cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression analyses of baseline data with complete demographic and smartphone ownership information.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Pediatrics, Volume 157, Issue 2, February 2026, e2025073891. Not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: All three conditions met: (a) Smartphone ownership age is DEPENDENT variable in all reported analyses; (b) ALL predictors are demographic/sociodemographic only (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, parental education); (c) Cross-sectional analysis only. No individual psychological/clinical/behavioral/neural outcomes examined. FT_EC7 applies per binding rule E14 (sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM, cross-sectional).
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria FT_IC1-IC6. FT_EC7 applies: smartphone ownership is sole dependent variable with only sociodemographic predictors in cross-sectional design.
